data.title: Coupang: Beginning A Monetization Cycle
data.description:
Introduction

I recently covered MercadoLibre (MELI) Best Growth Company In Latam, and while compiling a peer comparison table, two stocks stood out for growth and valuation: Coupang (NYSE:CPNG) and SEA (SE). Thus, I dove deeper into Coupang to better asses its potential. While the Korean e-commerce space is competitive and well penetrated, CPNG's low profitability is beginning to change. It can drive cash flow and earnings that have not yet been factored into valuations.

What is Coupang

The company came late to e-commerce, starting operations in 2010, well behind the leader, NAVER, which began in 1999. The key to its revenue success is extreme customer service, along the lines of Amazon (AMZN), the company has speedy and free delivery, with an extensive distribution and fulfillment center that currently reaches 70% of the population next day and is slated to reach 88% by 2027 via 100s of small distribution centers. It added 7 am produce delivery, same-day delivery, and a membership service called WOW, along with food delivery and payment services.

CPNG is primarily a 1P or first-person retailer; it buys, resells, and delivers merchandise. The 3P or marketplace service is newer but growing rapidly as small merchants and producers utilize the massive distribution system.

In 2024, the company acquired Farfetch, a luxury e-commerce retailer that was near bankruptcy. This has reduced margins as CPNG attempts to make the model work. It also expanded its e-commerce system to Taiwan. In its disclosure, these are classified as Developing Offerings.

The Korean E-Commerce Market

The South Korean e-commerce market is one of the most penetrated, accounting for 30% of total retail sales. It is also highly competitive, with hundreds of companies striving to capture a 50 million population, of which 83% use the internet for purchases. Growth rates have slowed to under 5% and are only slightly above total retail sales growth. The easy gains are largely behind it, and the battle for market share will continue, with consolidation already occurring. Coupang and NAVER account for around 24% of this market. They are growing GMV in the low teens, which both should be maintained as their respective scales (distribution/purchasing power) allow them to gain share vs traditional retail or other peers. I believe this is no longer a high revenue growth market, but CPNG has substantial momentum to achieve, given the massive gap in margins vs NAVER.

The Monetization Opportunity

In the last 10 years, Coupang has driven growth, revenue, and market share to be the leader in the Korean e-commerce sector. It has done this via extremely excellent customer service, with competitive prices and free and fast delivery, as discussed earlier. This has been at the expense of margins and cash flow, similar to Amazon. As the distribution capacity is built, sales growth slows, and the market consolidates with CPNG as the leader or co-leader, margins should rise rapidly from 3% to 7% by 2027, driving EPS by 4x.

Below, I compare NAVER to CPNG, illustrating the company's monetization opportunity and how Amazon's retail margins developed over time.

Consensus Forecast

The consensus estimates indicate a sizable increase in margins that will drive earnings growth above 30% annually through 2027, while revenue growth slows. At the same time, capex is flat, which will help produce an estimated US$2bn in FCF in 2026. The company should utilize this cash in some share buybacks or by acquiring shares from Soft Bank (27%).

Q1 2025 Results

The Q1 2025 results are broadly in line with consensus, demonstrating good growth and improving FCF generation. The key to the price rally was announcing a US$1bn share buyback program and guidance of 20% revenue growth in the Korean market.

Valuation

The consensus price target is US$30, which backs into an implied 34x P/CE (cash earnings: net income +depreciation + stock-based compensation) or 0.9x PEG. This is not an aggressive valuation, possibly due to the company's weak earnings and cash flow generation as it ramps up distribution, while the Farfetch acquisition was highly questionable. There is room for multiple expansions, but even at 1x PEG, the upside may be exceptional if/when CPNG can monetize its network/scale.

Peer Comps

Using consensus data for a wide selection of global peers, it appears that CPNG is not extraordinarily cheap but does have low margins with plenty of room to increase them, which drives one of the highest CE growth rates.

Risk

The main risk to the Buy rating is if the company cannot raise margins due to competitive reasons in Korea, or, far worse, results in Farfetch or Taiwan. Macro risk can also hurt this monetization drive; a weak Korean consumer may result in lower demand/sales, and margins. Finally, the Chinese e-commerce players may become more aggressive as they search to offset US sales. The Trade war is negative for CPNG.

Conclusion

I rate CPNG a Buy. After 15 years, the company has attained a leading share in the Korean e-commerce market via exceptional service that is not close to monetization. As margins rise from 3% to 7%, I believe EPS can increase by 4x by 2027 and drive shares higher.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Fed Holds Rates, In No Hurry To Cut
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Fed keeps rates on hold amid stagflation concerns. (0:16) Chairman Powell stresses the Fed can wait to see how tariffs shake out. (1:09) White House ready to kill the AI diffusion rule. (2:55)

The following is an abridged transcript:

The Federal Reserve had no surprises for the market as it kept interest rates unchanged at 4.25% - 4.5%. The FOMC members voted unanimously, a possible show of unanimity and support for Chairman Jay Powell amid pressure from President Donald Trump to lower rates.

In the changes in its statement from March, the FOMC raised a worrying specter of stagflation, adding that it “judges that the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen."

Economist Justin Wolfers says: “The Fed didn't use the word 'stagflation', but that's what it's warning about. Never a good moment when your central bank says that it's worried about *both* higher unemployment and higher inflation. That's a problem that monetary policy alone can't solve."

Janney fixed income strategist Guy LeBas posted: "Offhand, we cannot recall a precedent for the FOMC formally increasing its risk assessment on both sides of its mandate, certainly not so starkly."

The stagflation worries hit stocks initially, with Treasury yields sliding at the same time. But Powell stepped up to the mic and seemed to allay some concerns by giving perhaps his most boring press conference ever.

Powell channeled former Fed chief Alan Greenspan’s talent of speaking a lot and saying nothing. The one message from his hour of Q&A was “wait and see.”

He said the FOMC is in a good position to be patient and see where tariffs land, with the cost of waiting fairly low and everyone in the Committee supporting waiting.

"There's so much uncertainty ... everyone is just waiting to see how developments play out," he added.

Powell acknowledged weakness in sentiment that has followed the broad implementation of tariffs, but also pointed out it's not showing up in the hard data.

Economist Betsey Stevenson said: “The Fed can wait because they can't change a decline in supply due to the disruption in imports. They can try to decrease demand for that reduction in supply to help reduce inflationary pressure, but they can't create jobs to make up for those lost due to the reduction in imported goods.”

Powell said at the presser that influencing demand would be a pretty poor way to deal with supply chain problems.

Pantheon Macro economist Ian Shepherdson says: "The Fed has no better idea than anyone else how the tariff shock is going to play out, not least because the biggest single unknown is what happens after the end of the 90-day delay to 'reciprocal' tariffs . The only sensible action for the FOMC right now is to do nothing."

Following the press conference, the odds of rates holding at the current level after the June meeting rose. But fed funds futures are still pricing in a quarter-point cut in July and three for 2025. The bond market ended fairly steady, with the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) just below 4.3%.

But it was a different story for the stock market, which looked like it was set for a last-hour drop for the 9th Fed Day in a row.

But chips flipped the script.

Bloomberg reported right before the close that the Trump administration is set to kill the so-called AI Diffusion rule as part of a broader plan to alter semiconductor trade restrictions.

Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), Intel (INTC) and shares of several other semiconductor companies moved higher following the report.

The AI Diffusion rule, enacted during the last days of the Biden administration, was designed to limit the spread of advanced AI technology outside the U.S., with the purpose to prevent foreign threats from accessing it.

The S&P 500 (SP500) closed up +0.43%. The benchmark index has been down as much as -0.50% to a session low post-Fed. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) rose +0.27%.

The Dow (DJI) was the winner of the session, up +0.70% thanks mainly to a nearly 10-point gain in Disney (DIS).

Disney rose 10% as it guided strongly for the full year, posted a double beat, and added more subscribers than expected on its streaming platforms for the second quarter. It also announced plans for its seventh theme park resort in Abu Dhabi.

data.title: RB Global, Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by RB Global, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: OppFi: Fantastic Q1 Results Support The Firm's Turnaround Story
data.description:
Most of the companies that went public during the early 2020s special purpose acquisition company ("SPAC") boom ended up being major disappointments. Demand for SPACs was much higher than the amount of high-quality supply which could be brought to market. This led to many SPACs which traded down 90% or more since then, with some even going bankrupt.

I had mentally written off personal lending company OppFi Inc. (NYSE:OPFI) as yet another of the failed SPAC deals. And who could blame me? OPFI's stock sank 80% in the year following the launch of its SPAC offering, after all.

But then, something unexpected happened. Oppfi managed to turn its ship around, and the stock has had a meteoric recovery since then:

Things hit a particularly frenzied pace earlier this year, with the stock reaching a 52-week-high of $17.73. Even with the recent pullback, however, shares are up more than 200% over the past 12 months and have quintupled off the 2023 lows.

My first instinct when looking at a chart like this is to wonder whether the stock has become dramatically overvalued. Oftentimes, these sorts of massive runs are followed by steep corrections. Many traders seem to have positioned for that outcome, as OPFI currently has a reported 17% of its stock float being shorted. Bears seem confident that OppFi is set to give back a large chunk of its gains. But, I think that view could be mistaken.

OppFi's Loan Losses: The Key Metric Driving Performance

OppFi's fall and then rebound as a public company can be largely traced to one core item on its income statement. Namely, the company saw a massive increase in provisions for loans losses starting in 2022. It seems that the company aimed to scale up its lending book quickly following the SPAC transaction and had issues keeping up from an underwriting and risk management standpoint. However, as credit metrics have started to improve, loan losses have stabilized and profitability has returned. How does this look in chart form?

Here is OppFi's operating income including provisions for loan losses since 2018, courtesy of Seeking Alpha. Note that the chart runs from right to left, with 2024 being the leftmost entry:

As you can see, the company was quite profitable in 2020, generating $87 million of operating income. This appeared to position the company well for its 2021 SPAC transaction. However, profitability dropped sharply in 2021 and then went all the way to an outright loss in 2022. In 2023, operating profit jumped back to $43 million, followed by a surge in 2024 to a record high $98 million operating profit.

The company has grown its top line every year. While revenues have seen fairly linear increases, however, the troublesome factor were those provisions for loans losses. Here are the provisions by year:

  • 2020: $82 million
  • 2021: $87 million
  • 2022: $236 million
  • 2023: $236 million
  • 2024: $205 million.

As you can see, there was a massive jump in provisions for loan losses in 2022. This jump far exceeded the company's revenue growth for that span, indicating a seemingly dramatic drop-off in average loan portfolio quality. However, provisions were flat for 2023, and actually decreased a bit in 2024. This was at the same time that revenues jumped from $418 million in 2022 to $481 million in 2024.

These figures get to the central risk and reward inherent to OppFi's business model. It makes loans to customers who cannot get loans at most other financial institutions. The company states that its mission is:

To facilitate safe, simple and more affordable credit access to the 60 million everyday Americans who currently lack traditional options while rebuilding their financial health.

Understandably, there is a great deal of risk that comes with folks that have had previous bankruptcies or other major negative financial events. That's reflected in OppFi's huge provisions and charge-offs for loan losses.

Indeed, at the worst of the storm, OppFi's net charge-offs hit a stunning 62% of its total average loan receivables in 2022. It's quite remarkable to find a lending business with a 62% net charge-off rate.

So, how can OppFi make money despite that feature of its business model? A slide from a previous OppFi investor deck explains the firm's value proposition:

As per OppFi's slide, almost all other financial alternatives for people with damaged credit histories have triple-digit annualized interest rates. OppFi presents its loans – which are in the 59% to 160% range – as being a substantially better deal for its borrowers than most other alternatives. Some analysts may view OppFi's lending as predatory in nature and at excessive interest rates. However, as shown by OppFi's past loan losses, this is a highly risky form of credit, so there is some defense for charging 100%+ APRs on this type of product.

Q1 Results Show Tremendous Improvement

OppFi's first quarter results were the best of both worlds as it pertains to credit exposure.

The company's average annualized yield increased by 630 basis points year over year to 135.8%. It's not like the company's former 129.5% average annualized yield is all too shabby, but 630 basis points of improved yield adds a lot of torque to the top line.

And, on the cost of risk side, OppFi's net charge-off rate as a percentage of total revenue decreased 1,330 basis points to 34.6% versus the prior year. That's an absolutely gigantic improvement in results, and it looks even better compared to where OppFi's charge-offs were back in 2022 and 2023.

As the slide below shows, OppFi has been able to manage this drastic improvement in portfolio quality while at the same time still growing revenues:

The company seemingly believes its improvement efforts are structural in nature and can be sustained, rather than simply being a result of better consumer credit conditions. I would tend to agree, as we haven't seen this sort of marked improvement in consumer credit performance at other firms during this quarterly earnings cycle.

OppFi maintained its prior full-year revenue guide of $563 million to $594 million. At the same time, it raised its adjusted net income estimate by more than 10% for 2025 and now sees adjusted EPS at a midpoint of $1.22 for the year.

This, in turn, would imply that the stock is around 8x forward earnings. That's fair, but not especially cheap for this space. Higher-risk consumer lenders like OneMain Financial (OMF) have a history of often trading at a single-digit P/E ratio. And OppFi's loan book and lending strategy is less proven than, say, OneMain's or Synchrony Financial's (SYF). So I wouldn't be in a rush to slap a double-digit P/E multiple on OppFi.

That's especially true as OppFi's net charge-off rate has been highly variable in the past, and it's quite possible that it will see much larger loan losses when a recession hits. Lending at an average annualized yield of 136% is an inherently risky endeavor, and I'd love to see how OppFi holds up in a more sustained economic downturn. That said, the firm's Q1 results were exceedingly strong, and I am quite impressed with the operational turnaround that this company has achieved.

OPFI Stock Verdict

To be frank, this company has quite a few characteristics that normally make me nervous. It came public via a SPAC from a not particularly well-known sponsor. OppFi's initial results as a public company were quite poor. And the loan book takes a truly massive amount of net charge-offs in the course of doing business. On paper, this seems like a risky thing to hold going into a potential recession, especially with the stock up more than 200% over the past year.

And yet, I can't argue with the actual numbers. OppFi has done an excellent job turning the business around since its poor reception from public markets a few years ago. It has tightened things up operationally and made significant improvements to its net lending spread. The recent 25 cent per share special dividend also shows that the company is willing to reward its shareholders as the business' fortunes have improved.

I would not be chasing OPFI stock if it rallies sharply following the upbeat Q1 earnings. The stock has already moved so dramatically over the past year. That leaves less margin for safety heading into a macroeconomic environment where consumers are likely to face much more strain on their personal balance sheets.

That said, I am at a hold rating on OPFI shares for now. The company's remarkable operational turnaround makes OppFi interesting even at the much higher share price as compared to last year.

data.title: ZEQT:CA: Why BMO's Fund Has Underperformed Its Competitors
data.description:
As I continue to analyze and provide insights into all-equity funds in Canada, it's only natural that I discuss the BMO All-Equity ETF (TSX:ZEQT:CA). Out of the four EQT funds of ZEQT:CA, XEQT:CA, VEQT:CA, and FEQT:CA, BMO's ZEQT:CA has almost always underperformed XEQT:CA and FEQT:CA. Surface analysis would be to look at these longer-term total returns and choose the fund with the highest total returns. The flaw with this strategy, however, is that your investment, which was once performing well, could suddenly stop performing well, making it challenging to react accordingly unless you understand what is underneath the hood for these funds. ZEQT:CA has underperformed other competitor funds, but it does not necessarily make the fund unworthy in one's portfolio. The reason is that ZEQT:CA's underperformance has largely come from a higher weighting in "emerging" market assets and a lower weighting in "developed" markets relative to the other funds. There is a strong growth case, particularly revolving around AI and semiconductors in emerging markets such as Korea, Taiwan, China, India, Malaysia, and more, that could absolutely justify holding ZEQT:CA over the other EQT funds. That said, investors should recognize that a strong, growing economy does not always equate to stock market growth due to the potentially complex investing environment associated with many emerging markets. Thus, I rate ZEQT:CA a Hold.

ZEQT:CA's Holding Discrepancies

Unlike popular passive index investing strategies, the all-equity funds in Canada do have discrepancies that can greatly change the performances between each fund. It's not like choosing an investment in ZSP:CA versus VFV:CA, where both simply track the S&P 500 by holding its constituents. That said, there are similarities between all of these all-equity funds that have led them to have similar total returns aside from FEQT:CA, which has a unique crypto component. All four funds allocate about 40-50% to U.S. equities and 25-30% to Canadian equities. Aside from FEQT:CA, which has more specific financial-based selection methodologies, the other EQT funds typically take very similar approaches to create these U.S. and Canadian allocations. VEQT:CA, for example, holds a U.S. total market fund for its U.S. allocation. XEQT:CA does the same but holds the U.S. total market fund in combination with a small allocation in an S&P 500 ETF. ZEQT:CA opts for most of its weighting in an S&P 500 ETF, while adding small allocations in U.S. mid-cap and small-cap ETFs. As for Canadian allocations, ZEQT:CA and XEQT:CA take the same approach of holding an S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index ETF, while VEQT:CA opts for a FTSE Canada All Cap Index ETF. These differences have minimal effect on overall performances.

The funds will also hold international equities in developed markets outside of North America, which most often includes Japan, Europe, and Australia (or frequently referred to as EAFE), and emerging market equities that generally include the rest of the investable world. It is in these two sectors that most of the discrepancies lie. The reason for ZEQT:CA's lag behind funds like XEQT:CA and FEQT:CA lies largely in its higher weighting in emerging market funds. Compare ZEQT:CA's 17.37% allocation in developed markets and 7.88% in emerging markets to XEQT:CA's 26.35% and 5.07% allocations in developed and emerging markets, respectively. Similarly, VEQT:CA has about the same allocations in developed and emerging markets as ZEQT:CA, which is why their performances have been much closer, with the slight difference coming from VEQT:CA's 30.52% weighting in Canadian markets versus ZEQT:CA's 26.18%. Given how emerging market ETFs have largely underperformed developed markets in the last couple of years, it explains ZEQT:CA's underperformance relative to XEQT:CA and FEQT:CA.

The Bull Case For ZEQT:CA

ZEQT:CA's larger weighting in emerging markets, however, is also the reason for a potential investment in ZEQT:CA over other all-equity funds. The fund only launched in January 2022, after all, and if you refer to the chart comparing total returns for ZEA:CA and ZEM:CA, you'll see that emerging market ETFs have actually done quite well against developed markets prior to 2023. There is a strong case to believe that emerging markets can outperform other developed markets and potentially even the U.S. and Canadian markets in the next few years. This means investors more bullish on the performance of emerging markets will find ZEQT:CA more attractive than some of the other EQT funds.

Potential outperformance from emerging markets stems from AI and semiconductors. We've already seen earlier this year that China's AI development is not far behind the U.S. via what happened with DeepSeek and recent Nvidia chip bans could reduce Nvidia's market share in China and give Chinese competitors such as Huawei increased control over the region. On the topic of chip-making, you cannot forget that Taiwan is a part of these emerging market funds, with Taiwan Semiconductor's stock occupying large portions of these ETFs. Moreover, news of Malaysia becoming a semiconductor manufacturing hub to potentially replace Taiwan amid political tensions is another reason to invest in emerging markets. And of course, you cannot discount how countries with large growing populations, such as India and Indonesia, have so much development opportunity, especially in the face of "first-world" countries who all seem to be facing problems of an aging population that could lead to less economic growth due to a decline in the workforce.

The Risks of This Thesis

What must be noted, however, is that growth in a country's economy does not necessarily equal growth in its stock market. Part of the reason why the U.S. markets have seen some of the most growth is that their markets have much more favorable and simple rules relative to other countries. Additionally, the U.S. culture for stocks and companies is for corporations to make as much money as possible, rather than what you see in some other countries, where the government's role is much more pronounced. Huawei, for example, does not even trade on a public exchange, so if it were the only company to benefit from the U.S. ban on Nvidia chips, retail investors wouldn't be able to capture that growth effectively.

Additionally, it is important to note that emerging markets covers a broad category of countries. Buying ZEQT:CA off the idea that emerging markets will benefit from AI is not necessarily the strong play, as the regions benefiting are mainly China and Taiwan. Other stocks traded in regions included in the index, such as South Korea, may not necessarily benefit as much. Investing in ZEQT:CA with emerging market trends in mind is only really a strong play relative to investing in the other EQT funds.

Rating ZEQT:CA A Hold

Overall, I rate ZEQT:CA a Hold. Its underperformance since its inception relative to other EQT ETFs has largely resulted from its higher weighting in emerging market funds. There are reasons to believe that emerging market funds will perform better than developed markets again due to AI and chip-making potential in the regions included, but performance is not necessarily guaranteed due to the nature of stock investing in these countries. If you believe more strongly in the future long-term performance of emerging markets, then ZEQT:CA could be a great choice. The fund is tied for the lowest management expense ratio alongside XEQT:CA at 0.20%, and the higher weighting in emerging markets has only resulted in a 2% underperformance compared to XEQT:CA since ZEQT:CA's inception.

data.title: Buffett Passes The Torch: What Greg Abel Means For Berkshire's Future
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Warren Buffett took the stage in Omaha this year and this wasn’t just another Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (NYSE:BRK.B) shareholder meeting. It was, in many ways, a farewell.

The 2025 meeting was packed with timeless lessons and one very important practical implication.

The torch is being passed down to Greg Abel, Buffett’s handpicked successor.

  • Who is Greg Abel?
  • How does this impact Berkshire’s direction?
  • And what should investors consider as we move forward from the Buffett era?

Ultimately, it’s important to consider why Greg Abel has been chosen, which can give us a clue as to what the company values in its CEO.

Buffett's Final Lessons

At the tender age of 94, Buffett continues to be sharp-minded and quick witted. The soon-to-be former CEO of Berkshire conducted the annual meeting in a packed auditorium, where thousands gathered to listen to his words of wisdom.

Let’s look at some of the key takeaways from the meeting.

You don't have to swing at every pitch. You just have to wait for your pitch.

Source: Warren Buffett.

Buffett reiterated his patient approach to life and investing. This is especially relevant today as Berkshire holds a record amount of cash.

We don't see screaming bargains.

Source: Warren Buffett

Even after the selloff, Buffett continues to suggest the stock market is at least fairly valued. Of course, we will know a lot more about Berkshire’s positions soon when the 13F is released.

Tim Cook has made more money for Berkshire than I have/

Source: Warren Buffett.

A lot has been speculated about Berkshire’s trimming of the Apple (AAPL) position. Buffett defended the company, and emphasized that this was done to raise cash and remain flexible. Apple does remain after all of Berkshire's top holding.

Lastly, Buffett spoke on tariffs too, the topic du jour:

The more prosperous the rest of the world becomes, it won’t be at our expense. The more prosperous we'll become—and the safer we'll feel, and your children will feel someday.

Source: Warren Buffett.

In line with what most CEOs have stated, Buffett said the tariffs were a big mistake. However, Berkshire is doing its best to mitigate this risk, by staying vertically integrated and production.

The Greg Abel Era Begins

The most important takeaway is the change in leadership that will take place at the end of the year.

Greg Abel will replace Buffett as CEO. Abel is already overseeing all the non-insurance parts of Berkshire, which accounts for two thirds of the business, so this seems like a natural transition.

Abel started his career as an accountant at PwC, and then later moved into the energy sector as he began to work for CalEnergy.

He meets people and they’re instant friends. He’s not loud or bombastic, but he’s 500% friendly. No one’s better at hosting a party than Greg. He makes sure everyone has a good time—not in an over-the-top way, but by making it personal.

Source: Wells Fargo Exec.

Abel is not loud or flamboyant, but he has been praised for his brilliance, candidness and being trusted. All very Buffett-worthy attributes.

Understandably, though, a lot of investors aren’t thrilled that Buffett is leaving. Not only has Buffett led Berkshire to consistent returns for decades, but his name carries a premium that few can replicate.

Berkshire’s stock has in fact sold off since the announcement, but not by a significant amount.

What to Expect From Berkshire

Berkshire faces a new set of challenges: rising rates, inflation, global fragmentation, and a scale that makes it hard to move the needle.

I talked in-depth about these dynamics in my last Berkshire post, where I explained how the company was positioning for a 1970s like environment.

I would argue that Abel’s appointment actually backs up some of these ideas.

The biggest defining characteristic that defines Abel, in my opinion, is his experience in the energy sector.

This is very interesting, because Berkshire has already been slowly accumulating large energy positions in companies like Occidental petroleum (OXY) and Chevron (CVX).

As the head of Berkshire Hathaway Energy, Abel has already been behind some key investments in renewable energy, grid modernization and some large-scale infrastructures.

With energy production and distribution likely becoming a significant bottleneck to AI expansion, Berkshire’s choice in CEO could turn out to be incredibly prescient.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, though, it's important to keep in mind that the value and philosophy that made Berkshire great will persist. Furthermore, Buffett will remain on the board of Berkshire, and any big moves that happen in the coming quarters will no doubt get the Buffett seal of approval.

I remain bullish on Berkshire, and I am very excited to see what the next 13F filing brings us.

Market Overview

US equities registered their first quarterly loss since the third quarter of 2023. Volatility surged and risk sentiment waned as markets grappled with considerable uncertainty surrounding the new administration’s policies on trade, immigration, fiscal spending, tax cuts, deregulation, and escalating tariffs. Against a turbulent market backdrop, technology indices fell sharply. Growth and small-cap stocks significantly underperformed their value and large-cap counterparts, respectively.

Global fixed-income markets generated positive total returns during the first quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index. Trade tensions and monetary-policy divergence drove market volatility and an increasingly clouded economic outlook. Most fixed-income sectors underperformed government bonds as spreads widened amid an expected deteriorating growth/inflation tradeoff. The US dollar depreciated vs. most currencies following the US administration’s policies aimed at reducing the trade deficit.

Performance Summary

Hartford Balanced Income Fund (I Shares) outperformed its benchmark.

Security selection was the main driver of relative outperformance due to strong selection in industrials, healthcare, and consumer discretionary.

Sector allocation also contributed to relative results, primarily driven by our overweight positions in utilities and energy.

Our underweight allocation to investment-grade credit contributed over the quarter as spreads widened broadly. Our security selection in the sector also modestly contributed.

Our overweight to high-yield credit detracted over the quarter as spreads widened in this segment of the market.

Our security selection in emerging markets contributed. Despite credit-spread widening in the sector, the Fund was able to generate positive benchmark-relative returns in this sector.

Positioning & Outlook

Our universe remains rich with new opportunities. We continue to focus on finding high-quality businesses with strong balance sheets and sustainable dividends. We’re spending a lot of time on our downside stress-test scenarios and are confident in the sustainability of dividends and long-term value of holdings in the portfolio.

Within equities, utilities, energy, and healthcare represented our largest sector overweights relative to the benchmark at quarter-end. We were most underweight to financials, communication services, and industrials.

Within the fixed-income portion of the Fund, we maintain our underweight positioning in investment-grade credit as valuations remain relatively rich compared to history. We maintain a modest overweight to emerging markets as we find attractive relative value in this segment of the market.

1Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

data.title: Fed To Hold Constant
data.description:
The Federal Reserve emphasized two things in the report it released following the FOMC meeting of today.

First: Uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further.

Second: The risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen.

The future is cloudier...and, the economy has moved into a state of stagflation.

Neither is desirable.

In terms of its primary monetary policy tool:

The FOMC "will continue reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities."

And, to support the Federal Reserve goals of maximum employment and inflation at the rate of 2 percent over the longer run, the FOMC "will maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25 to 4.50 percent."

In other words...the Federal Reserve will continue its monetary policy set for the foreseeable future.

Of particular interest to me is the Fed's assessment of economic activity: the Fed's assessment is that "economic activity has continued to expand at a solid pace."

And, how does the Fed get to this position?

After all, in the first quarter of 2025, the economy declined!

But, the Federal Reserve discusses the first quarter in this way. From the fourth quarter of 2024 through the first quarter of 2025, the economy declined by 0.3 percent, or, by (the actual decline between the two periods) times 4.

That is the actual decline was the quarterly decline times 4: the actual decline was 0.075 percent...hardly anything.

(The year-over-year growth rate was a positive 2.0 percent!)

The item that pushed the first quarter into a decline was the growth of imports due to the changing tariffs being assessed. Imports are subtracted from exports and this gave the change a negative sign...but a very, very small one.

In other words, if one takes off the value of the decline in exports, the rest of the real GDP figure for the first quarter of 2025 is positive...and a pretty strong positive.

In other words, except for imports, the performance of real GDP in the first quarter of 2025 was a pretty good one.

As the Fed presents in its Press Release, "economic activity has continued to expand at a solid pace."

"The unemployment rate has stabilized at a low level in recent months, and labor market conditions remain solid."

"Inflation remains somewhat elevated."

These descriptions could be applied to the state of the U.S. economy during the entire year of 2024.

The fear...as captured at the start of this article...greater and greater uncertainty combined with the possibility of growing stagflation.

And, this is the fear that the FOMC faces for 2025 and beyond.

For now, the Fed faces a relatively strong employment picture. It is the inflation future that appears to be the most threatening.

The Fed, right now, faces a major threat of losing the fight against inflation. As mentioned above, already there is fear that inflation is getting out of control again. And, one of the major consequences of the imposition of tariffs is the additional stimulus this pressure will further put on prices.

The Fed has fought for more than three years to bring U.S. inflation under control. It has done a very good job so far. It does not want to walk away from the battle quite yet.

The Federal Reserve will continue to fight to keep some sense of control over prices.

But, given the uncertainty that now exists in the marketplace, one cannot say that forces will not be released in the upcoming months that set off inflation again.

I think the Fed is going to do all it can to finish the battle against inflation it began over three years ago.

I believe that this is the message that the current press release of the Fed's Federal Open Market Committee is trying to get out.

Personally, I hope that the Fed and the FOMC can succeed.

data.title: Valuing Alphabet On A Sum Of The Parts Basis, Assuming Search Stops Growing
data.description:
Google Search Under Assault?

It seems that headlines constantly are talking about Google Search and how at risk the business is. Adding insult to injury, Apple’s Eddy Cue at Google’s antitrust trial testified that search queries on Apple phones fell for the first time in April.

He went on to say that chatbots from Perplexity or Anthropic could be replacements for Google Search on iPhones. We know that ChatGPT is also an option and all are generally cited as potential disrupters to Google’s enormously profitable search engine business.

Given the drubbing in Google stock, we thought it made sense to revisit the valuation on a sum of the parts basis. Many may or may not realize that Google is a tech platform with a variety of impressive assets, which we will break down and value separately.

The core business, Google Search, has seen growth rates fall on tough comps and on the law of large numbers. But even within Google searches, click-through rates have been in decline as many are finding answers within Google’s own AI summary boxes (in their Search Labs AI Overview at the top of Google searches).

Per the above, website publishers are seeing large declines in click-throughs, as Google searches are putting AI putting answers front and center. That somewhat obviates the need to click further.

And ChatGPT user growth has been phenomenal. While data is a bit conflicting, sources suggest that ChatGPT has between 400-800 million users (some weekly, some monthly). They hope to get to 1 billion users by the end of 2025.

On the plus side, GOOG launched Gemini 2.5 Pro last month. Reviews suggest that it is the best AI model out there.

Here was one take.

Source

Betting markets agree.

People seem to forget that Google was not the early mover in search. AskJeeves and Yahoo and a number of other search engines came first.

Personally, I use Copilot and Gemini and have little recent experience with ChatGPT (as I found it useless for financial analysis a while back). I still use basic searches daily.

Still, ChatGPT has the early mover advantage and while Gemini is growing, they only have 350 million users compared to 400-800 million at ChatGPT. And a lot of those are probably AI Labs results in their basic search, not necessarily dedicated nor many paying Gemini users.

Google doesn't exactly break this out for investors.

But what if we are wrong? What if Google Search gets really disrupted by Chatbots?

First of all, while Apple search queries might not have grown in April, it is unclear that that extends to all browsers. I recently added Google directly to my iPhone and have been searching on that instead of Safari.

Below is a chart illustrating Google search query growth over time.

In the most recent quarter, Google’s Search business grew revenue 10% year over year to $50.7 billion in revenue. Last year Search generated $198 billion in revenue out of $350 billion. That is 56.5% of revenue (or 50% excluding Traffic Acquisition Costs, TAC, which includes fees that Google pays to Apple to put its browser as the default).

So, definitely a concern if half their revenue is disrupted.

Below we see Search revenue (within Google Services).

Search market share is holding up well, despite ChatGPT’s being around for 2.5 years now.

Since October 2022, Google search share has fallen from 92.3% to 89.7%. If there were real disruption, shouldn’t this share be a lot lower? Or shouldn’t the number of queries be falling and revenue declining?

Anecdotally, I mostly use Google Search (and get annoyed when somehow Explorer turns Bing into my default search engine; instantly I can tell that search results are lower quality). The quality of results remains high.

While there is a lot of concern that chatbots will eliminate users googling, AI Search on Google has shown a recent jump in user satisfaction.

Search and Sum of the Parts

As we built a sum of the parts model, we dove into the numbers at Search. We observe that the Search segment within Services includes a LOT of stuff.

From the 10-K: “Google Services' core products and platforms include ads, Android, Chrome, devices, Gmail, Google Drive, Google Maps, Google Photos, Google Play, Search, and YouTube, with broad and growing adoption by users around the world.”

Within Services, Google breaks out Search revenue but not operating income, so we made some assumptions there. A lot of Google's products are free, like Gmail, Docs/Sheets, Maps, etc. Ads there do not seem at risk.

Outside of Search there is YouTube, Waymo, Google Cloud and a variety of Other Bets in other segments which we valued separately.

Recently, in a DOJ case, the court ruled that Google monopolized its ad technology (or tech for its auctions that brings together online advertisers as well as website publishers).

While above our pay grade, Google owns ad servers in the middle (and data for both publishers/advertisers). GOOG forces both to use their tech, and it is unclear exactly what the remedy is. The company points out that nobody is forcing these companies to do business with Google, and customers seem mostly happy.

So there is some breakup risk to Search/Google from this case.

We already talked about the antitrust case from last year for subscribers in some detail. From that case, there are decent odds of a ruling that Google is forced to divest Android or Chrome (where the company collects a TON of data for its advertising businesses).

But even in a divestiture, I am not sure why I would not use Google searches and Chrome. Would results really be that different? Would advertisers flee Google if they cannot provide browsing data? Search effectiveness would certainly be compromised.

But as Meta Platforms showed us, there are workarounds to track user behavior that I am sure Google is working on diligently. Chrome is free, and there could be a licensing agreement to pay for data in a separation or spin off.

In both court cases, Google will appeal until the end of time, meaning any material impact is probably years away (and will be negotiated).

Not surprisingly, Yahoo and ChatGPT have expressed interest in buying Chrome. The DuckDuckGo CEO said Chrome was worth $50 billion.

If they dismantle Chrome and search suffers, it won’t go away per se. Especially with all of the other valuable pieces within Search. I still think Google Maps is one of the greatest inventions from the 2000s, and advertising there could really become large.

Search may not be as effective for advertisers but still might prove far better than Bing for users.

And if Apple migrates to using Chatbots over search, would they be able to collect $20 billion a year in fees from say Perplexity to entirely replace Google? That is a lot of money to walk away from, and my sense is that Apple will want to offer Safari users AI and a high-quality search engine at the end of the day.

Downside Valuation / Search Disruption

To really stress Google's valuation, we put a 5x estimated EBITDA multiple on Search and assumed it stopped growing until the end of time.

For other segments, we assumed YouTube / Google Play subscriptions are worth $634 billion (with analysts suggesting YouTube alone is worth up to $575 billion).

In Cloud, we put a 18x EBITDA multiple on current EBITDA, where Microsoft is trading (but note that Google Cloud is growing faster than MSFT is overall and so is probably worth 20-25x).

Waymo is worth at least $75 billion, plus Google has $81 billion of cash.

Putting a $20 billion liability for courtroom drama/fines, and we peg the sum of the parts downside at $133 per share.

On 2026 estimates, assuming zero Search growth but continued growth in Cloud and YouTube, and we get a value of $143 per share.

That suggests 12% downside in a year and 5% downside on 2026 estimates.

The margin of safety is looking pretty good right now for those with a couple of years of patience. Big picture, Google is trading at 16x 2025 earnings estimates net of cash. That is about as low as it has ever been, and we can point to a lot of lower quality, far slower growth companies trading at 25-30x.

Again, $133-143 assumes a dismally low value for Search at 5x EBITDA and gives zero credit for Google’s Gemini business or its quantum computing technology.

In contrast, Meta Platforms (META) today, after its large decline, is trading at 12x forward EBITDA (and usually trades at 14.7x).

So, in our view, 5x is REALLY stupidly conservative.

Here are details on each segment with our assumptions.

Below are recent results.

Quarterly Results Refresher

These are Google’s quarterly results.

Apples to apples EPS grew 21% YoY. Cloud revenue grew 28%, Cloud margins almost doubled to 17% and YouTube ads grew 10%.

As mentioned, Search also grew 10%. Overall revenue ex-currency grew 14%.

Capex is expected to be $75 billion this year, with buybacks of $70 billion (3.5% of the float) planned.

Street estimates are $9.50 for 2025, but $8.75 excluding the SpaceX gain booked in Q1 2025.

2026 consensus estimates appear too high to us at $10.19.

In our upside case, we assume AI chatbots and search co-exist, that EPS grows at a 10% rate in 2026-2027 and EPS climbs to $10.50.

At 22x 2027 figures, an arguably conservative multiple given their collection of assets, and we see fair value of $232 (53% upside in 2–3 years).

We view recession risk and the law of large numbers as perhaps bigger concerns at Google. Already, some Asian direct sellers in the U.S. have stopped advertising on their platform (due to tariffs). Further economic weakness could put a dent in EPS growth this year. 2026 estimates will probably come down.

But so far, the numbers tell a story of continued growth with a lot of incredibly valuable businesses.

Strong buy, but tempered by recession concerns.

data.title: Trump Tariffs Trigger Collapse In China-To-U.S. Shipping Volumes
data.description:
If you’ve been inside a Walmart, Target, or Home Depot lately, you may not realize that a trade war is underway between the U.S. and China, the world’s two largest economies. Store shelves are well stocked, and prices have largely held steady.

All of that is set to change very soon if forecasts turn out to be correct.

Logistics firm Flexport reports that liners are slashing their China-to-U.S. carrying capacity at a faster rate than they did at the start of the pandemic.

Meanwhile, data provided by container ship tracker Vizion shows that bookings from China to the U.S. are dropping sharply due to President Donald Trump’s 145% levy on Chinese-imported goods. In the week ended April 21, liner vessels bound for U.S. West Coast ports were carrying only 90,831 TEUs (20-foot equivalent units), which is close to half the volume from a year earlier.

Gene Seroka, executive director of the Port of Los Angeles—the United States’ busiest port—told Bloomberg that “import-export with China is very limited right now.”

Economists Warn of Recession as Job Cuts Accelerate

Below is a near-term timeline of projected events, based on analysis from Torsten Slok, chief economist at Apollo Global Management. By mid-May, the first container ships will arrive at West Coast ports since President Trump announced “Liberation Day” tariffs on April 2. As previously noted, these ships will be carrying significantly less cargo than usual, resulting in empty shelves later this month.

Consequently, layoffs in the trucking and retail sectors are expected by late May or early June as shipping demand collapses. FreightWaves reports that job cuts have already begun, with up to 1,800 freight-related positions eliminated across Alabama, Florida, Georgia, Tennessee, and South Carolina.

Mr. Slok expects the U.S. economy to fall into recession this summer, and it’s worth mentioning here that real GDP contracted slightly in the first quarter, the first time it’s done so since the pandemic. A decline in Q1 was earlier projected by the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model, though a contraction of 2.4% was expected.

The International Monetary Fund (IMF) has slashed nearly 1% from its forecast for U.S. growth in 2025. The institution now believes the U.S. economy will advance only 1.8% this year.

Back-to-School and Christmas Inventory Face an Uncertain Future

Even once differences between the U.S. and China are ironed out—whenever that may be—it could take weeks for trade to normalize and goods to be fully restocked. Inventory for back-to-school and holiday sales is at risk, Flexport says.

The Toy Association, a U.S.-based trade group, is warning that Christmas 2025 could be in jeopardy. Nearly 80% of toys sold in the U.S. are manufactured in China. The Association notes that 96% of American toy companies are small or mid-sized businesses, and about half say they could go out of business in the coming weeks or months without immediate relief from the new tariffs.

Short-Term Pain May Lead to Long-Term Industrial Gains

The president and his advisors say that his protectionist trade policies will spur manufacturers to return to the U.S. His commerce secretary, Howard Lutnick, has described a future where U.S. citizens work in factories “for the rest of your life, and your kids work here, and your grandkids work here.”

Since Trump took office four months ago, a number of companies have announced plans to invest and expand their presence in the U.S. These include Apple, Honda, IBM, Merck, NVIDIA, and Taiwanese semiconductor maker TSMC.

My belief is that if Trump manages to bring these kinds of jobs back to the U.S. permanently, the short-term pain due to tariffs may be worth it in the long run. China joined the World Trade Organization (WTO) in December 2001, and since then, the U.S. has seen an overall decline in manufacturing jobs and output. Manufacturing as a percentage of the U.S. economy is now below 10%. That’s down from about a quarter of the economy in the 1950s.

What Should Investors Do?

It may not surprise you to learn that gold glitters as my safe haven of choice for investors concerned about economic and financial turmoil surrounding Trump’s trade policies. I’m clearly not the only one who feels this way.

Inflows into gold-backed ETFs have remained positive in each of the four months of the year so far. From January 1 to April 30, a net 6 million ounces of gold were added to these ETFs’ holdings. Meanwhile, the precious metal rallied 25.3% year-to-date through the end of April, marking the best first four months of the year since 2006, when it climbed 26.6%.

I believe there could be further upside potential as long as Trump’s tariffs remain in place, increasing investor demand for stability.
